coredata.dc:title: Tumor-specific fluorescent Cdots-based nanotheranostics by acid-labile conjugation of doxorubicin onto reduction-cleavable Cdots-based nanoclusters
coredata.dc:creator: Pei, Mingliang
Li, Guoping
Liu, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (Cdots) have attracted more and more interests in bioimaging and tumor theranostics. However, their practical application has been limited due to the small particle size and non-tumor-specific fluorescence. Here, reduction-cleavable disulfide-linked Cdots-based nanoclusters were fabricated to conjugate doxorubicin (DOX) via an acid-labile hydrazone bond. Owing to the pH and reduction dual-stimuli responsiveness, the proposed Cdots-based nanotheranostics possessed unique tumor-specific fluorescent property and tumor-specific controlled drug release performance, indicating their promising potential for the in-situ real-time fluorescent monitoring of therapeutic response in future tumor therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent nanotheranostics
Tumor-specific
Carbon quantum dots
pH and reduction dual-stimuli
Disintegration

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of amino-riched CDots
In vitro cellular toxicity and uptake

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) CDots, (b) SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters and (c) SSCDots -Hy-DOX nanotheranostics.

Typical hydrodynamic diameter distributions of CDots (a), SSCDots nanoclusters (b), SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters (c) and SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics in aqueous dispersion (d), ssCDots-Hy-DOX in 7.4 PBS (e) and cell culture medium (f) for 7 days, and (g) the SSCD-Hy-DOX nanoclusters dispersion in pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH solution for different times (0 h, 4 h, 8 h, 60 h).

(A) FTIR spectra of CDots (a), SSCDots (b) and SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters (c), and SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics (d), and (B) UV–vis spectra of SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters, SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics and DOX in aqueous system.

Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) CDots (0.01 mg/mL) and (b) SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters (0.01 mg/mL) under different excitation wavelengths, and (c) SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics (2 mg/mL) in pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH for different times at λex = 350 nm.

Digital images of (a) CDots (0.01 mg/mL), (b) SSCDots nanoclusters (0.01 mg/mL), (c) SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters (0.01 mg/mL), and (d) SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics (0.01 mg/mL) in aqueous dispersions under daylight and UV light at 365 nm.

Digital images of free DOX (0.01 mg/mL) (a) in aqueous solution, SSCDots-Hy (0.01 mg/mL) in 10 mM GSH at 48 h (b) and SSCDots-Hy-DOX (0.01 mg/mL) in pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH at 48 h (c) under daylight and UV light at 365 nm.
pH/Reduction dual-responsive DOX release from the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics in different releasing media.

Cell viability of HepG2 incubated with the SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters, free DOX and SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics (A) and HaCaT cells and HepG2 cells incubated with the ssCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics (B) with different concentrations.
Confocal microscopy images of HepG2 cells with free DOX (15 μg/mL) and SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics (15 μg/mL DOX equiv./mL) after incubation for 8 h.
Confocal microscopy images of HaCat cells with SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics (15 μg/mL DOX equiv./mL) after incubation for 8 h.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of the proposed tumor-specific SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics.
pH and reduction dual-responsive disintegration and DOX release of the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics.
Fitted DOX release parameters with Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas models.

head.ce:title: Tumor-specific fluorescent Cdots-based nanotheranostics by acid-labile conjugation of doxorubicin onto reduction-cleavable Cdots-based nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (Cdots) have attracted more and more interests in bioimaging and tumor theranostics. However, their practical application has been limited due to the small particle size and non-tumor-specific fluorescence. Here, reduction-cleavable disulfide-linked Cdots-based nanoclusters were fabricated to conjugate doxorubicin (DOX) via an acid-labile hydrazone bond. Owing to the pH and reduction dual-stimuli responsiveness, the proposed Cdots-based nanotheranostics possessed unique tumor-specific fluorescent property and tumor-specific controlled drug release performance, indicating their promising potential for the in-situ real-time fluorescent monitoring of therapeutic response in future tumor therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoscaled drug delivery systems (DDSs) have been intensely investigated for the on-demand drug delivery in the cancerous tissues or cells [1]. To conveniently understand their pharmaco-kinetic properties or evaluate their therapeutic effect, the imaging diagnostic function has been integrated into the DDSs in the form of nanotheranostics [2], by the incorporation of magnetic nanoparticles [3], gold nanoparticles [4], carbon nanoparticles [5], quantum dots [6] or fluorescent dyes [7].

Owing to the excitation-dependent emission, high fluorescence quantum yield, long photoluminescence decay lifetime, and photostability, carbon quantum dots (Cdots) have been widely used for various applications [8], especially in the biomedical field for chemotherapy and/or photodynamic therapy with fluorescence imaging capability [9,10]. However, the Cdots suffer from the rapid renal clearance by the reticuloendothelial systems (RES) in the body due to their too small particle size. Furthermore, most of the Cdots-based nanotheranostics did not possess the specificity for the tumor intracellular microenvironment, that is to say, the nanotheranostics exhibited fluorescence in both normal physiological medium and tumor intracellular microenvironment [11–29]. So their fluorescence imaging could only be used to specify the biodistribution of the theranostic agents in both normal and tumor tissues. To achieve the in-situ real-time fluorescent monitoring of therapeutic response, tumor-targeting groups were usually introduced into the theranostic agents, facilitating the tumor accumulation [11,27].

By now, it is still a challenge to develop smart Cdots-based nanotheranostics with different fluorescent properties in the normal and tumor tissues. Namely, they are expected to show a “off-on” fluorescence during internalization by the tumor cells, or fluorescence with different emission wavelengths after internalization, responding upon the tumor intracellular signals, such as pH and reduction. Owing to the tunable optical properties of Cdots by doping or modification [30], the pH-sensitive Cdots have been intensely reported. However, most of them showed strong fluorescence at high pH media but very weak or no fluorescence at low pH media (tumor acidic intracellular environment) [31–33], restricting their practical application in tumor imaging. Although the emission wavelength red shift [34,35] and fluorescent intensity increase [35] have been reported for sensor applications with declining the media pH, there is no report on the tumor-specific fluorescent Cdots-based nanotheranostics by now, to the best of our knowledge.

Here, smart Cdots-based nanotheranostics with unique tumor-specific fluorescent property were designed by conjugation of doxorubicin (DOX) onto the reduction-cleavable disulfide-linked Cdots-based nanoclusters via an acid-labile hydrazone bond (Scheme 1 ). The proposed SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics was stable in the normal physiological media with very low premature drug leakage and very weak fluorescence. As in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment, the conjugated DOX could be released in the acidic media, meanwhile, the reduction-cleavable Cdots-based nanoclusters could disintegrate into separated Cdots particles. That is, the proposed Cdots-based nanotheranostics with very weak fluorescence, because of the fluorescent quenching caused by the covalently crosslinking and DOX conjugation, could emit strong fluorescence after drug releasing and carrier disintegrating in the tumor intracellular microenvironment, demonstrating a promising potential for the in-situ real-time fluorescent monitoring of therapeutic response in future tumor therapy.

Sodium citrate and ethylenediamine were analytical pure reagents, obtained from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Company. Acryoyl chloride (96%) and cystamine hydrochloride (˃97%) were purchased Tianjin Heowns Company and Alddin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai), respectively. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX·HCl, 99.4%) was obtained from Beijing Huafang United Technology Co., Ltd. Hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O, ˃ 80.0%) was bought from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute. All other reagents and solvents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Double distilled water was used throughout.
The amino-riched carbon quantum dots (CDots) were prepared by the microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and ethylenediamine, as reported previously [27].

N,N-bis(acryloyl)cystamine (BACy) was synthesized by the amidation of cystamine with acryloyl chloride according to the procedure reported previously [36]. 1H NMR (400 MHz): δ 8.26 (s, 2H), 6.18 (dd, J = 17.0, 10.1 Hz, 2H), 6.05 (dd, J = 17.0, 2.3 Hz, 2H), 5.56 (dd, J = 10.1, 2.3 Hz, 2H), 3.38 (dd, J = 12.8, 6.5 Hz, 4H), 2.78 (t, J = 6.8 Hz, 4H) (Fig. S1).

The reduction-cleavable carbon quantum dot-based nanoclusters (SSCDots) were prepared via a facile Michael addition reaction between the amino-CDots and the disulfide-based diacrylate (BACy). Simply speaking, amino-CDots (0.50 g) and BACy (0.10 g) were added into anhydrous dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (30 mL) in flask and allowed to react for 32 h at room temperature in dark. The SSCDots nanoclusters were collected by dialysis (MWCO of 1000) against water for three days and freeze-drying, as brown powder.

The hydrazine modified SSCDots nanoclusters (SSCDots-Hy) were prepared by the reduction of SSCDots nanoclusters with hydrazine hydrate [37]. In general, the SSCDots nanoclusters (65 mg) was dispersed into 50 mL of DMSO, and then 10 mL of N2H4·H2O was added with stirring at room temperature in dark. After the reaction for 24 h, the brown SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters were collected by dialysis (MWCO of 1000) against water for 3 days and freeze-drying.

The SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics were prepared by conjugating DOX onto the SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters with acid-labile hydrazone bond. Typically, the SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters (60 mg) was dispersed into 30 mL of DMSO. After 20 mg of DOX, 20 μL of trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) and 200 μL of triethylamine (TEA) were added, the mixture was bubbled with N2 gas for 30 min and stirred in dark at room temperature for 24 h. The proposed SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics were collected by centrifugation (10,000 rpm for 5 min), dialysis (MWCO of 14,000) against water for 7 days and freeze-drying.

The in vitro DOX release from the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics was evaluated by dialysis method, in different simulated releasing media: pH 7.4 phosphate buffered saline (PBS), pH 5.0 acetate buffered solution (ABS), pH 7.4 PBS with 10 μM GSH, and pH 5.0 ABS with 10 mM GSH, respectively. The SSCDots-Hy-DOX (10 mg) dispersion in 10 mL of above releasing media was dialyzed (MWCO of 14,000) in 110 mL of the corresponding releasing solution with shaking at 120 rpm at 37 °C. After certain times, 5 mL of the releasing solution was taken out to measure the DOX concentration on a UV–vis spectroscopy at 480 nm, according to the standard calibration curve of DOX in the corresponding releasing solution. Simultaneously, 5 mL of the corresponding releasing solution was added to ensure the volume of the releasing medium constant.
1H NMR spectra were recorded with a Nuclear Magnetic Resonance instrument (JEOL ECS 400 M) at 400 MHz with the solvent of dimethylsulfoxide-D6.
Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was acquired with a Nicolet 360 FT-IR spectrometer with wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1, with the KBr pellet technique.
UV adsorption spectra of the samples were recorded with a UV–vis spectrometer (TU-1902) at room temperature, with their aqueous dispersions.

Fluorescent emission spectra of the amino-CDots, SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters and SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics were detected with a Hitachi F-7500 fluorescence spectrometer, with their aqueous dispersions.
The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of the samples were measured on a FLS920 Quantaurus-QY measurement system, with their aqueous dispersions.
The elemental analyses were performed with an Elementar Vario EL instrument (Elementar Analysensysteme GmbH, Munich, Germany).

Morphologies of the amino-CDots, SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters and SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics were characterized on a JEM-1200EX transmission electron microscopy (TEM), sampling with their aqueous dispersions.

Hydrodynamic diameters of the samples were measured with their aqueous dispersions on a dynamic scattered light system (DLS, BI-200SM) at room temperature. The average hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) was presented as a mean value of three measurements with RSD < 3%.
The zeta potentials of the CDots and nanoclusters were determined using a Nano Brook 90 Plus PALS (Brookhaven Instruments Corp., Holtsville, NY, USA) in their aqueous dispersions.

MTT assay was performed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics against a human liver cancer cell line (HepG2 cells), in comparison with the SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters and free DOX, and a normal human keratinocyte cell line (HaCaT cells). The cells with density of 1 × 104 cells/well in 96-well culture plates were cultured overnight, then the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics, the SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters or free DOX were added at different concentrations (0, 5, 10, 15, 20 μg/mL). After further incubation of 24 h, the MTT solution (0.5 mg/mL) was added to each well and co-incubated in a CO2 incubator for 4 h. Then the MTT-containing medium were removed and replaced with 100 μL of DMSO. After the crystal substances were dissolved for 20 min in each well, the absorbance was read with a microplate reader at 490 nm.

The cellular uptake, intracellular drug distribution and cancerous cell imaging of the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics in the HepG2 and HaCat cells were evaluated with the confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) technique. In the assay, the cells seeded overnight in a glass-based 24-well plate were treated with fresh medium containing SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics (15 μg/mL DOX equiv./mL) or free DOX (15 μg/mL), and incubated at 37 °C for 8 h, respectively. Then the cells were fixed with glutaraldehyde (2.5%) and the cellular nuclei were stained with DAPI. After rinsing with PBS, the fluorescence images of the CDots and Dox were conducted on a CLSM instrument (DMI 4000B, LEICA, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of amino-riched CDots
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent amino-riched CDots were synthesized via the one-pot microwave method, which using sodium citrate as carbon source and ethylenediamine was used as modifier to provide rich amine groups and surface passivation [27]. Then the surface-active amine groups of as-prepared amino-riched CDots were crosslinked with BACy via Michael addition reaction to produce the CDots-based nanoclusters (SSCDots) with desired particle size. Finally, the proposed SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics were obtained by conjugating a model chemotherapeutic drug DOX via an acid-labile hydrazone bond onto the SSCDots nanoclusters after reduction with hydrazine (SSCDots-Hy) (Scheme 1).

The morphology and size of the obtained CDots were characterized with the TEM and DLS techniques. The as-prepared CDots are nearly spherical and well dispersed, with an average size of 3 nm in the TEM image (Fig. 1a). While a bigger hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) of 3.5 nm was obtained from the DLS analysis (Fig. 2a), maybe due to the swelling of the surface polar groups on the CDots.

The CDots displayed a broad peaking at approximately 21° in the XRD pattern (Fig. S2a), indicating the highly disordered carbon with a predominantly amorphous structure. The Raman spectrum of the CDots showed two peaks around 1325 and 1520 cm−1 (Fig. S2b), attributed to the D band (sp3-hybridized) and G band (sp2-hybridized), respectively. The results demonstrated the successful synthesis of the CDots with graphite carbon phase. As shown in Fig. S2c, the CDots exhibited two absorption bonds: one at 350 nm likely due to n − π* transition and the other at 238 nm due to the formation of multiple aromatic structure to might creating π − π* transition [27]. Furthermore, the CDots showed a Zeta potential of −10.4 mV in its aqueous dispersion (Fig. S3), indicating its negatively charged surface because of massive surface carboxyl groups.

In the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 3A), the broad absorbance peak at about 3400 cm−1 corresponded to the OH and NH stretching vibrations, the characteristic absorbance of carboxyl CO group at 1770 cm−1 [27], the amide CO stretching vibration peak from a different chemical environment at 1700 cm−1, the aromatic CC and amide CO stretching vibrations around 1660 cm−1, the bending vibration of NH at 1560 cm−1, the characteristic of CN stretching vibration of amine at 1290 cm−1, and the COC in stretching mode at 1160 cm−1 revealed the existence of carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups [38].

The fluorescent emission spectra of the CDots (0.01 mg/mL) with different excitation wavelengths from 330 to 430 nm in aqueous dispersion are shown in Fig. 4a. The CDots possessed the excitation-independent fluorescence property, with maximum PL intensity at excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of the amino-riched CDots was determined as 15.8% under water solution (excitation wavelength at 350 nm; emission wavelength at 400–600 nm). In addition, the aqueous dispersion of the amino-riched CDots (0.01 mg/mL) showed strong blue fluorescent intensity under 365 nm UV (Fig. 5a).

Then the CDots-based nanoclusters (SSCDots) were fabricated by crosslinking the amino-riched CDots with BACy via the Michael addition (Scheme 1). The SSCDots nanoclusters showed a larger Dh of 212.8 nm (Fig. 2a). The characteristic CH stretching vibration increased (Fig. 3A) and a sulfur content of 0.65% was found in the products after the Michael addition, also revealing the successful crosslinking of the amino-riched CDots into the reduction-responsive SSCDots nanoclusters. Because of the Michael addition reaction from the surface amino groups on the amino-riched CDots, the zeta potential of the SSCDots nanoclusters further decreased to −17.8 mV (Fig. S3). The PLQY of the SSCDots nanoclusters decreased to 2.09%, much lower than the amino-riched CDots, which might be due to fluorescent quenching caused by the covalent crosslinking [30].

To render the acid-labile drug conjugation, the hydrazine modified SSCDots nanoclusters (SSCDots-Hy) were prepared by the reduction of the SSCDots nanoclusters with hydrazine [37], as shown in Scheme 1. The SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters showed near spherical shape with an average size of 25 nm in the TEM analysis (Fig. 1b) and a slightly smaller Dh of 183.3 nm than the SSCDots nanoclusters in the DLS analysis (Fig. 2c) due to the decreased hydrophilicity after amidation. The characteristic absorbance of carboxyl CO group decreased (Fig. 3A) while the Zeta potential of the products increased to −13.5 mV after the reduction, demonstrating the successful amidation of the surface carboxyl groups of the SSCDots nanoclusters during reduction with hydrazine, as reported previously [37]. The SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters showed the similar UV adsorption as the amino-riched CDots (Fig. 3B).

The SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters maintained the blue fluorescence at excitation wavelength of 350 nm, although the PL intensity declined (Fig. 4b). The PLQY of the SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters was calculated to be 3.21%. Furthermore, the PL intensity of the SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters were not affected by the pH values of the aqueous dispersions and treating with GSH (Fig. S4 a and b). It is to say, after disintegration with GSH, the separated CDots showed the similar fluorescent property as the SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters.

Finally, DOX was conjugated onto the SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters via the acid-labile hydrazone linkages. The appearance of the CH bending vibration of the aromatic ring in DOX at 1573 cm−1 and the stretching vibration of the anthracene ring in DOX at 538 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of the product demonstrated the successful DOX conjugation (Fig. 3A) [19]. In the UV–vis adsorption spectrum, the adsorption at 480 nm of DOX appeared for the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics, besides the two adsorption peaks of the SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters (Fig. 3B). The DOX content in the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics was measured as 15.7% by UV–vis at 480 nm with corresponding standard curve (Fig. S5), after DOX had been completely released in an extreme condition (1.0 M HCl aqueous solution) for 24 h [39].

The SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics showed the irregular shape with much bigger particle size of around 50 nm (Fig. 1c), might be due to the aggregation of the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanoclusters during the sampling because of the enhanced hydrophobicity after DOX conjugation. However, a Dh of 142.3 nm was obtained from the DLS analysis (Fig. 2d), smaller than the SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters. The results indicated that the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics could be well dispersed into water, although the enhanced hydrophobicity declined their swelling degree. Also due to the enhanced hydrophobicity, the Zeta potential of the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics increased to −9.3 mV (Fig. S3). Furthermore, no obvious change could be seen in the hydrodynamic diameter and distribution of the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics after treating with pH 7.4 PBS and cell culture medium for 7 days (Fig. 2 e and f), demonstrating their excellent stability.

The fluorescence of DOX could also be seen in 550–600 nm in the fluorescence emission spectrum of the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics, also revealing the successful DOX conjugation, but their PL intensity decreased in comparison with the SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters (Fig. 4c), with a slightly lower PLQY of 2.81%. However, it was found that the PL intensity of the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics increased gradually with prolonging the treating time with pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH. The enhanced PL intensity of the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics after reduction-triggered disintegration, via cleaving off the disulfide crosslinkage in the nanotheranostics similar as reported previously [16], might be used for the tumor-specific in-situ real-time fluorescent monitoring.

To explore the tumor-specific fluorescence of the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics, which could recover to the SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters upon the acid-triggered DOX release, reduction-triggered disintegrate into separated DOX modified CDots, and pH/reduction dual-triggered disintegrate into separated CDots residues (Scheme 2 ), the fluorescence emission behaviors of the SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters in presence of GSH and the SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters in presence of DOX were investigated. After treating with 10 mM GSH for 48, the PLQY of the SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters increased to 3.01%, indicating the disintegrated product, the separated CDots residues, possessed stronger fluorescence emission than the crosslinked nanocluster.

For an aqueous dispersion of the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics (2 μg/mL) in the pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH medium, in which the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics could be broken off into the separated CDots residues and DOX molecules, the PL intensity increased gradually with prolonging the treating time, mainly due to the formation of the separated CDots residues, although the fluorescent quenching of the CDots with DOX [40] as well as the aggregation-caused quenching [41] via the FRET process (Fig. 6 ). However, the fluorescent quenching occurred in presence of DOX with high concentration (Fig. S4c). Taking account of the DOX content of the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics (15.7%), the released DOX concentration should be much lower than the separated CDots residues, so the slight fluorescent quenching could be ignored. On the other hand, the released DOX molecules and the separated CDots residues should be accumulated in different organelles after cellular endocytosis and tumor-specific disintegration, DOX in cell nucleus while the separated CDots residues in cytoplasm, such fluorescent quenching is expected to be avoided in the practical application of the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics.

The pH/reduction dual-triggered DOX release performance from the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics was evaluated using dialysis method. As shown in Fig. 7 , the cumulative release of DOX was 14.4% in pH 7.4 PBS in 75 h, while it reached 31.5% in pH 5.0 ABS. The results demonstrated the pH-triggered DOX release from the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics. Furthermore, the drug leakage of 14.4% in pH 7.4 PBS indicated that some DOX might be loaded onto the SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters via non-covalent interaction, such as π˗π stacking, hydrogen bonding, and/or electrostatic interactions. Such non-covalent drug-loading resulted in the fluorescent quenching of the CDots (Fig. 6) [40,41].

Then the DOX release profiles in the simulated physiological medium (pH 7.4 + 10 μM GSH) and the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment (pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH) were compared. The DOX releasing rates in the two simulated media were faster than those without GSH, revealing the reduction responsive drug release. All the four drug release profiles showed fast drug releasing rates in the first 8 h, mainly due to the non-covalent drug-loading. The drug release reached equilibrium within 75 h in the simulated physiological medium similar as the pH 7.4 releasing medium, with a low cumulative release of 15.8%. While the DOX release profiles in simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment showed a near linear sustained drug release after the first 8 h, giving a cumulative release of 70.4% in 105 h. The drug releasing rate in the sustained drug release stage was much higher than that in the pH 5.0 ABS medium without GSH.

In the acidity of pH 5.0, the hydrazone linkage should be cleaved off to release DOX. So the difference in the drug releasing rates with or without GSH should be caused by the diffusion effects: the diffusion of H+ into the nanotheranostics to cut off the hydrazone conjugation, and the diffusion of the released DOX out of the nanotheranostics. To reveal the different diffusion effects, the hydrodynamic diameters of the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics in the corresponding media were traced with DLS technique. The Dh increased gradually from 140 nm to about 160 nm after treating with pH 5.0 ABS for 48 h, indicating the swelling the nanotheranostics during the DOX release because of the enhanced hydrophilicity. Conversely, the Dh increased to 250 nm and 370 nm within 4 h and 8 h respectively, with a unimodal distribution, while a region with diameter of few nanometers appeared along with a region with diameter of 400–1100 nm after 60 h (Fig. 2g). The diameter variation demonstrated the reduction-triggered disintegration of the SSCDots nanoclusters during the pH-triggered drug release, as shown in Scheme 2. In the early stage, the hydrophilicity of the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics increased with the drug release, and the crosslinking degree of the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics decreased because some of the reduction-responsive disulfide crosslinkage was cleaved off, so the hydrodynamic diameter increased (4 h and 8 h). Then the nanoclusters with low crosslinking degree disintegrated to produce the separated CDots residues, with the similar diameter as the pristine CDots (60 h).

The DOX release mechanism from the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics was also investigated with the Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas models (Fig. S6). The fitted data, including the regression coefficient (R2), kinetic constant (k) and release exponent (n), are summarized in Table 1 . Most of the regression coefficients obtained from the Higuchi model were higher than those from the Korsmeyer-Peppas model, indicating the drug release mechanism could be better described with the Higuchi kinetic model of the Fickian diffusion mechanism [42]. The kinetic constant (k) increased with increasing the acidity or GSH concentration in the releasing media, also demonstrating the pH/reduction dual-triggered drug release performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cellular toxicity and uptake
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of the blank SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters and the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics were assessed using MTT assay, in comparison with the free DOX. As shown in Fig. 8A, the SSCDots-Hy nanoclusters showed no obvious cytotoxicity towards cancer cells (HepG2 cells) with the cell viabilities ˃ 98%, even under a high dosage of 20.0 μg/mL. The SSCDots-Hy-DOX hybrid nanotheranostics showed significantly cytotoxicity against HepG2 cells in the dose-dependent mode, similar as the free DOX. The SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics possessed a half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of 14.70 μg/mL, near to the value of the free DOX (13.28 μg/mL), although the cumulative release from the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics was only 25% within 8 h. The MTT assays indicated the enhanced tumor growth inhibition of the proposed SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics than the free DOX, owing to the different of cell internalization mechanism from the free DOX via diffusion, the P-glycoprotein (P-gp) efflux pump inhibition of the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics via endocytosis [43].

As for the normal cell line (HaCaT cells), the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics also showed obvious dose-dependent cytotoxicity, despite lower than the cancer cells (Fig. 8B). It should be due to the slower intracellular pH-triggered DOX release in the normal cells with much lower GSH level than in the cancer cells with high GSH concentration.

Finally, the cellular internalization and intracellular biodistribution of the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics were characterized with the CLSM technique after incubation of the HepG2 cells for 8 h, in comparison with the free DOX with the same equivalent dosage (15 μg/mL). As shown in Fig. 9 , the red fluorescence of DOX completely covered the blue fluorescence of the cell nucleus stained with DAPI, indicating the free DOX had been internalized into the cancerous cells, accumulating in both the cell nucleus and cytoplasm [44].

The CLSM images also revealed the successful cellular internalization of the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics, with the similar intracellular DOX biodistribution as the free DOX. Additionally, the strong green fluorescence of the CDots appeared after pH-triggered DOX release and reduction-triggered disintegration into the separated CDots residues which possess strong fluorescent intensity, mainly in the cell cytoplasm (Fig. 5d). It further revealed that the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics could be internalized by HepG2 cells and disintegrated into the separated CDots residues in the cytoplasm [16]. Such fluorescence of the CDots is expected for the in-situ real-time fluorescent monitoring of therapeutic response in future tumor therapy.

The similar results were also obtained for the normal cells, HaCat cells (Fig. 10 ), meaning the SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics could be internalized by the HaCat cells, in which the conjugated DOX could be pH-triggered released in the acidic intracellular microenvironment and accumulated in the cell nucleus. Comparing the green fluorescence of the CDots in the two cell lines, it could be found that the green fluorescence was uniform in the cancer cells, but obviously uneven in the normal cells, indicating that the SSCDots could not be disintegrated in the normal cells due to the much lower GSH level.

In summary, tumor-specific fluorescent SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics were designed for the tumor chemotherapy and imaging diagnostic, by conjugating DOX via an acid-labile hydrazone linkage onto the reduction-cleavable disulfide-linked Cdots-based nanoclusters (SSCDots-Hy). The proposed SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics with very weak fluorescence could disintegrate into separated CDots residues with strong fluorescence and DOX molecules in the tumor intracellular microenvironment, owing to the pH/reduction dual-stimuli responsiveness, thus possessing unique tumor-specific fluorescent property and tumor-specific controlled drug release performance. The smart all-in-one therapeutic effect/imaging diagnostic function makes the proposed tumor-specific fluorescent SSCDots-Hy-DOX nanotheranostics promising potential for the in-situ real-time fluorescent monitoring of therapeutic response in future tumor therapy.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Mingliang Pei:Methodology, Investigation, Data curation.Guoping Li:Investigation.Peng Liu:Conceptualization, Visualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province, China (Grant no. 18JR3RA271).
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2020.110719.
